blocks.content: The Copper Mug smelled like coffee, toasted sugar, and whatever Mr. Alvarez called wisdom when he was trying to sell day-old muffins.
Ethan sat at his usual table near the window, pencil in hand, sketchbook open.
He had been there for twenty minutes.
He had drawn the fountain six times.
Not well.
That was the annoying part.
He could draw buildings. Faces, sometimes. Hands, badly, but with effort. Trees if he didn't overthink them.
But the fountain refused to become a fountain.
Every version looked like he was trying to draw the idea of waiting.
Ethan stared at the page.
The pencil moved again.
A curve for the stone rim.
A thin line for the water.
The suggestion of the bench behind it.
Then, without permission, a figure standing near the easel.
He stopped.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: No,
he whispered.
Across the café, Mr. Alvarez looked up from polishing a mug.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: No what?
Ethan closed the sketchbook too quickly.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Nothing.
Mr. Alvarez's eyebrows rose with the patience of a man who had heard many terrible lies and found all of them useful.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Nothing has been very active this morning.
Ethan took a sip of coffee. It was too hot. He suffered quietly because admitting pain would invite conversation.
Mr. Alvarez walked over anyway.
Of course he did.
He stopped beside Ethan's table and looked at the closed sketchbook.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: You are abusing that pencil.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I'm drawing.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: No. You are interrogating paper.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Do you have customers to emotionally harass?

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: You are my customer.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I paid already.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: That only covers the coffee. The emotional harassment is complimentary.
Ethan tried not to smile and failed a little.
Mr. Alvarez glanced toward the sketchbook again.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: You do not usually draw the same place twice.
Ethan's fingers tightened around the cup.
He should have known Mr. Alvarez would notice.
The man noticed everything.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: It's just a fountain,
Ethan said.
Mr. Alvarez looked at him over the top of his glasses.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: No.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: No?

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: It is never just the thing.
Ethan looked down.
The words landed too close to something he did not want named.
Mr. Alvarez gave him one more second of mercy, then tapped the table lightly with two fingers.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: When the same place keeps returning to your hand, pay attention.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Is that advice or something you read on a tea bag?

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Both can be true.
Ethan laughed under his breath.
Mr. Alvarez turned to leave, then paused.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: And Ethan?

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Yeah?

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: If that place has red hair, perhaps draw slower.
Heat climbed Ethan's neck so fast it should have required medical attention.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I'm never coming here again.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: You say this every Thursday.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: It's Wednesday.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Then I am early.
Mr. Alvarez walked away, looking extremely pleased with himself.
Ethan opened his sketchbook again, mostly to prove he was not embarrassed.
He was absolutely embarrassed.
The fountain waited on the page.
Empty bench.
Thin water.
Something unfinished.
He touched the pencil to the paper.
This time, he did not erase the figure.
